answer:
**The key section defining *cetana* (Conscious Principle) and *acetana* (Unconscious Principle) appears in Chapter 1 of *Vṛhaspatitattva*, beginning around verse 6.**[^1]

Īśvara teaches Vṛhaspati that the supreme *tattva* (reality/principle) is twofold: *cetana* and *acetana*. *Cetana* is defined as having the nature of knowledge (*jñāna*), eternally aware, unobscured, and never forgetting. *Acetana*, by contrast, lacks knowledge entirely, likened to a stone.[^2] Their relationship is foundational: when *cetana* and *acetana* "meet," they generate all subsequent *tattvas* (*pradhānatattva*, *triguṇatattva*, etc.).[^3]

The text elaborates that *cetana* (e.g., Śivatattva) pervades *acetana* (e.g., Māyātattva, which is void and unconscious), interweaving it (*ūtaprota*) yet becoming "stained" by impurity (*mala*, equated with unconsciousness). This diminishes *cetana*'s omniscience and omnipotence, contracting it into bound souls (*ātmā*, "dazed consciousness").[^4]

A later potter-clay analogy reinforces this: the unconscious (*acetana*, like clay) is shaped by the conscious (*cetana*, like the potter/Lord) according to will, underscoring *cetana*'s primacy while *acetana* is passive and dependent.[^5]

[^1]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-04d69c).
[^2]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-c31df3).
[^3]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-c31df3).
[^4]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-643885).
[^5]: *Vṛhaspatitattva*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28/01/translation/#p-a00f4f).
